---
title: "The Thread class — creating and managing workers"
---

> *"Okay, I believe a worker thread is real now — I saw it print its own name and id. But how do I actually **control** one? How do I pass it data, get a result back, wait for it, run a dozen of them, or set one running quietly in the background? And why does my program sometimes hang forever when it should just exit?"*

[Chapter 1](01_what_is_a_thread.qmd) answered *what a thread is*. This chapter is the hands-on follow-up: **how you create, configure, and steer workers** with Python's `threading.Thread` class. By the end you'll know every method on a `Thread`, the two ways to build one, the traps that bite everyone (calling `run()` instead of `start()`; programs that won't exit), and you'll have written the **first thread of our running example — a data producer** that every later chapter builds on.

::: {.callout-tip}
## The running example for this whole part

From here on, one system carries every chapter — a classic **producer/consumer data pipeline**:

> **producer thread → a bounded queue → a pool of worker threads → results streamed over a socket → clients.**

It's a general shape that shows up everywhere: a log/event ingester, a web-scraping pipeline, a job-processing server, a sensor-data collector. We build it one thread and one primitive at a time, finishing with a runnable socket-streaming **capstone**. The cells that *execute here* use a tiny in-memory item source so the threading behaviour is real and reproducible; the socket/streaming pieces are shown as source you run in your own script. This chapter builds the **producer** — the thread at the head of the pipeline.
:::

## The whole picture — a `Thread` object is a remote control

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
    MK["<b>Make it</b><br/>Thread(target=fn, args=…)<br/>— or subclass and override run()"]
    MK --> ST["<b>.start()</b><br/>spawns the OS thread,<br/>puts it in Ready, calls run()<br/><i>(call ONCE)</i>"]
    ST --> AL["<b>while it runs</b><br/>.is_alive() → True<br/>.name · .ident · .native_id"]
    AL --> JN["<b>.join()</b><br/>caller BLOCKS until it finishes<br/>.join(timeout) → wait at most N s"]
    JN --> DN["<b>finished</b><br/>.is_alive() → False<br/>read results off the object"]
    AL -.->|"daemon=True"| BG["<b>background worker</b><br/>killed abruptly when<br/>main thread exits"]
    classDef mk fill:#2196F3,color:#fff;
    classDef act fill:#4CAF50,color:#fff;
    classDef warn fill:#F44336,color:#fff;
    class MK,ST mk;
    class AL,JN,DN act;
    class BG warn;
```

The mental model: a `Thread` **object** is not the thread itself — it's a **remote control** for one. You build the remote, press `.start()` to launch the actual worker, then use the other buttons (`.join()`, `.is_alive()`) to monitor and wait for it. Keep that separation in mind; it explains most of the surprises below.

## 1. Two ways to build a worker

There are exactly two ways to give a thread something to do.

**Way A — pass a target function (the common one).** You hand `Thread` a function to call. This is what you'll use 95% of the time.

```{python}
import threading

def produce_one(item_id):
    me = threading.current_thread()
    print(f"  [{me.name}] produced item {item_id}")

worker = threading.Thread(target=produce_one, args=(0,), name="Producer")
worker.start()
worker.join()
```

**Way B — subclass `Thread` and override `run()`.** You make your own class, put the work in a `run()` method, and `start()` calls it. Use this when the worker needs to **hold state** (counters, buffers, a stop flag) — which our producer will.

```{python}
import threading

class GreetingThread(threading.Thread):
    def __init__(self, who):
        super().__init__(name=f"Greeter-{who}")   # ALWAYS call super().__init__()
        self.who = who                            # state lives on the instance

    def run(self):                                # start() will call this
        print(f"  [{self.name}] hello from {self.who}")

t = GreetingThread("Ada")
t.start()
t.join()
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## The #1 subclassing bug: forgetting `super().__init__()`

If your `__init__` doesn't call `super().__init__()`, the `Thread` machinery is never set up, and `.start()` raises `RuntimeError: thread.__init__() not called`. Always call it first. (And put your work in `run()` — never name your method `start`.)
:::

**Which to use?**

| Use **target function** when… | Use **subclass** when… |
|---|---|
| the work is a simple callable | the worker carries **state** (counters, buffers, a stop flag) |
| you want the least ceremony | you want named methods like `.stop()` on the worker |
| (most cases) | the worker *is* a long-lived component (our producer/worker threads) |

Our pipeline threads carry state and a stop switch, so the running example uses **subclassing**. For one-off jobs, reach for `target`.

## 2. Passing data *in* — `args` and `kwargs`

A thread's function rarely takes zero arguments. You pass them when you build the `Thread`, **not** when you start it:

```{python}
import threading

def process(item_id, *, mode):
    print(f"  processing item {item_id} in '{mode}' mode")

# positional args go in a TUPLE; keyword args go in a DICT
t = threading.Thread(target=process, args=(7,), kwargs={"mode": "edges"})
t.start()
t.join()
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## `args` must be a tuple — the one-argument comma trap

`args` is a tuple of positional arguments. For a **single** argument you still need the trailing comma: `args=(7,)`. Writing `args=(7)` passes the *integer* `7`, not a one-tuple — Python then tries to unpack `7` as the argument list and you get a confusing `TypeError`. `(7,)` is a tuple; `(7)` is just `7` in parentheses.
:::

## 3. Getting data *out* — threads don't `return`

Here's a wall everyone hits. A thread's target can `return` a value, but **nobody receives it** — `start()` doesn't hand it back, and there's no `worker.start()` result.

```{python}
import threading

def compute():
    return 42                # this return value goes NOWHERE the caller can see

t = threading.Thread(target=compute)
t.start()
result = t.join()            # join() ALWAYS returns None — not 42
print(f"what join() gave us: {result}")
```

So how *do* you get a result out? Three ways, in increasing sophistication:

1. **Store it on the thread object** (clean with a subclass): the worker writes to `self.something`, and after `join()` you read it. Safe because `join()` guarantees the worker has *finished* before you read (a "happens-before" relationship — no race).
2. **A shared container** (a list/dict the worker appends to) — works, but if *multiple* threads write to it you've created a race condition (Chapter 3) and need a lock (Chapter 4).
3. **A `queue.Queue` or a `Future`** — the real tools, coming in Chapter 7.

Here's pattern 1, which our producer uses:

```{python}
import threading

class SumThread(threading.Thread):
    def __init__(self, n):
        super().__init__()
        self.n = n
        self.result = None              # a slot for the answer

    def run(self):
        self.result = sum(range(self.n))   # write the result onto myself

t = SumThread(1000)
t.start()
t.join()                                # wait until run() has finished...
print(f"result read off the thread: {t.result}")   # ...THEN read it safely
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## ▶ Why is reading `t.result` after `join()` safe, with no lock?

Because `join()` creates a **happens-before** ordering: everything the worker did in `run()` is guaranteed complete and visible *before* `join()` returns. You're not reading `t.result` *while* the thread writes it — you're reading it strictly *after* the thread is dead. A race needs two threads touching the same data *concurrently*; here the access is sequential (worker writes, then joins, then main reads). No overlap → no race → no lock needed. The moment you read shared data *while* a thread is still running, that reasoning collapses — which is exactly the next chapter.
:::

## 4. The lifecycle methods — every button on the remote

Now the full control surface. We'll provoke each one.

### `start()` vs `run()` — the trap that silently removes your concurrency

This is the most important distinction in the whole chapter.

- **`start()`** spawns a *new* OS thread and arranges for `run()` to execute *there*.
- **`run()`** is just a normal method. Calling it yourself runs the code in the **current** thread — **no new thread is created at all.**

Calling `run()` instead of `start()` doesn't error. Your program works — it's just secretly **single-threaded**. Watch:

```{python}
import threading

def show_where_i_run(tag):
    print(f"  {tag}: running in '{threading.current_thread().name}'")

print("with .start()  →", end=" ")
t1 = threading.Thread(target=show_where_i_run, args=("start",), name="Worker-A")
t1.start(); t1.join()      # prints 'Worker-A' — a real new thread

print("with .run()    →", end=" ")
t2 = threading.Thread(target=show_where_i_run, args=("run",), name="Worker-B")
t2.run()                   # prints 'MainThread' — NO new thread; ran right here!
```

The `.run()` line printed `MainThread`, not `Worker-B`: the work happened on the main thread. If your "threaded" code mysteriously isn't faster and everything runs in `MainThread`, this is almost always why. **Always `start()`.**

::: {.callout-warning}
## `start()` works exactly once

Calling `.start()` a second time on the same `Thread` object raises `RuntimeError: threads can only be started once`. A `Thread` object is single-use — to run the work again, build a new `Thread`. (This is why thread *pools* exist: to reuse worker threads without rebuilding them — Chapter 7.)
:::

### `join()`, `join(timeout)`, and `is_alive()` — waiting and supervising

`join()` blocks the *calling* thread until the target finishes. With a `timeout`, it waits **at most** that long, then returns whether or not the thread is done — so you check `is_alive()` to find out which happened.

```{python}
import threading
import time

def slow_job():
    time.sleep(0.5)

t = threading.Thread(target=slow_job)
t.start()

t.join(timeout=0.1)                 # wait up to 0.1s — NOT enough
print(f"after short join: alive? {t.is_alive()}")   # True — still going

t.join()                            # now wait as long as it takes
print(f"after full join:  alive? {t.is_alive()}")   # False — finished
```

`join(timeout)` is how you supervise a worker without blocking forever — "give it 5 seconds to shut down, then move on." We'll use exactly this for graceful shutdown later.

### Identity — `name`, `ident`, `native_id`

Recap from Chapter 1, now as configuration: set `name` when you build the thread (it's for *your* logs/debugging — it has no effect on behaviour). `ident` is Python's unique id while the thread is alive; `native_id` is the kernel's TID (what `top -H` shows).

```{python}
import threading

t = threading.Thread(target=lambda: None, name="Inspector")
t.start()
print(f"name={t.name}  ident={t.ident}  native_id={t.native_id}")
t.join()
```

## 5. Daemon threads — background workers that die with the program

By default a thread is **non-daemon**: Python will **not exit** until it finishes. That's why your script sometimes seems to "hang" at the end — a non-daemon thread is still running, and the interpreter politely waits for it.

A **daemon** thread is the opposite: it's a background helper, and when the **main thread exits, all daemon threads are killed instantly** — mid-line, no cleanup, no `finally` blocks guaranteed. You set it *before* `start()`.

```{python}
import threading
import time

def background_heartbeat():
    # a daemon: a tidy loop, but it will be cut off when main exits
    for i in range(40):
        time.sleep(0.05)

beat = threading.Thread(target=background_heartbeat, daemon=True)
beat.start()
print(f"is it a daemon? {beat.daemon}   alive? {beat.is_alive()}")
time.sleep(0.12)            # main does a little work...
print("main is about to exit — the daemon will be killed, not joined")
# (no join) — when this cell's program ends, `beat` is terminated abruptly
```

::: {.callout-important}
## The daemon trade-off — convenience vs. cleanup

- **Daemon (`daemon=True`):** the process can exit even if this thread is still looping. Great for *fire-and-forget* background helpers (a metrics poller, a heartbeat). **Danger:** it's killed *abruptly* — if it was writing a file or holding a resource, that work is interrupted with no chance to clean up.
- **Non-daemon (default):** the process waits for it. Safe for work that *must* finish, but a non-daemon thread stuck in an infinite loop will **hang your program on exit** forever.

The professional pattern is *neither extreme*: a non-daemon (or daemon) worker with an explicit **stop signal** (an `Event`) and a bounded `join(timeout)`, so it shuts down *cleanly and promptly*. We build that in Chapter 6; for now, just know: **"my program won't exit" = a non-daemon thread still running**, and **"my background work got cut off" = a daemon killed at exit.**
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## You can't change `daemon` after `start()`

Setting `t.daemon = True` after the thread is started raises `RuntimeError: cannot set daemon status of active thread`. Decide before you launch it (pass `daemon=` to the constructor, or set it before `.start()`).
:::

## 6. The running example — the producer

Now we assemble what we've learned into the head of the pipeline: a **producer thread** that generates work items and counts them, with a clean stop switch. It's a `Thread` subclass because it carries state (`items_produced`, the latest item) and a stop flag.

A real producer usually pulls from a **blocking source** — reading lines off a network socket, pulling rows from a database, waiting on a sensor or a message broker. Those reads *block*, which is exactly why the producer lives in its own thread: while it sits in *Blocked* waiting for the next item, the rest of the program stays responsive. Here's the shape, with the blocking source shown as a placeholder you'd swap for your real one:

```python
# producer.py  — sketch you adapt to your real source
import threading

class ProducerThread(threading.Thread):
    """Pulls items from some (blocking) source until asked to stop."""

    def __init__(self, source):
        super().__init__(name="Producer", daemon=True)   # background producer
        self.source = source            # e.g. a socket, a DB cursor, a broker client
        self.items_produced = 0
        self.latest_item = None
        # NOTE: don't name this `_stop` — threading.Thread already has an internal
        # `_stop()` method, and shadowing it with a bool breaks the thread. (We
        # upgrade this plain flag to a threading.Event in Chapter 6.)
        self._stop_requested = False

    def run(self):
        for item in self.source:        # iterating a real source BLOCKS between items
            if self._stop_requested:
                break
            self.items_produced += 1
            self.latest_item = item     # newest item, for others to read

    def stop(self):
        self._stop_requested = True
```

To **run and reason about it here in the book**, we plug in an in-memory source that yields items with a small delay (standing in for the blocking wait). The threading structure is *identical*; only the source changes:

```{python}
import threading
import time

class ProducerThread(threading.Thread):
    """Generates `n` work items, pausing between each to mimic a blocking source."""

    def __init__(self, n, interval=0.005):
        super().__init__(name="Producer", daemon=True)
        self.n = n
        self.interval = interval
        self.items_produced = 0
        self.latest_item = None
        self._stop_requested = False     # not `_stop` — see note in the source above

    def run(self):
        for i in range(self.n):
            if self._stop_requested:
                break
            time.sleep(self.interval)        # pretend the next item takes time to arrive
            item = {"id": i, "payload": i * i}   # a work item is just a small dict here
            self.items_produced += 1
            self.latest_item = item

    def stop(self):
        self._stop_requested = True

producer = ProducerThread(n=1000, interval=0.005)
producer.start()             # producer runs in the background
time.sleep(0.25)             # let it produce for a quarter second
producer.stop()              # flip the stop flag
producer.join(timeout=1.0)   # wait (briefly) for run() to notice and exit

print(f"produced ~{producer.items_produced} items in 0.25s "
      f"(expected ≈ {int(0.25 / 0.005)})")
print(f"latest item: {producer.latest_item}  alive? {producer.is_alive()}")
```

We started a background worker, let it produce, signalled it to stop, joined it with a timeout, and then safely read its results off the object (the §3 pattern). That's the entire Chapter-2 toolkit in one place — and the producer the rest of the pipeline will consume from.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## ▶ Q: The stop flag is a plain `self._stop_requested = True` — isn't that a race condition?

A fair worry, and the honest answer is: *for a single boolean flag set by one thread and read by another, in CPython, this particular case is safe enough* — assigning and reading a bool is atomic under the GIL (Chapter 3 makes "what's atomic" precise). But it's **fragile**: it relies on GIL specifics, gives no way to *wait* for the stop (the reader has to poll), and doesn't generalize. The robust, portable tool is `threading.Event` — same idea, but explicit, waitable, and correct without leaning on GIL trivia. We deliberately use the plain flag now and **upgrade it to an `Event` in Chapter 6**, so you see *why* the better tool exists before adopting it.
:::

## 7. Visualization — the lifecycle, and daemon vs. non-daemon at exit

The clearest picture of this chapter is *when each method takes effect* along a worker's life, and how a **daemon** vs **non-daemon** thread changes when the program can exit.

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Top: a non-daemon worker — main reaches the end but the program cannot exit until the worker finishes and is joined. Bottom: a daemon worker — main exits on its own schedule and the daemon is killed mid-work. The dashed red line is 'program exits here'."
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

COLORS = {"main": "#2196F3", "worker": "#4CAF50", "daemon": "#FF9800",
          "killed": "#F44336"}

fig, (ax_top, ax_bot) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(9, 4.2), sharex=True)

# --- non-daemon: program waits for the worker ---
ax_top.broken_barh([(0, 3)], (1.6, 0.6), facecolors=COLORS["main"])      # main runs
ax_top.broken_barh([(1, 5)], (0.6, 0.6), facecolors=COLORS["worker"])    # worker runs
ax_top.axvline(6, color=COLORS["killed"], ls="--", lw=2)                 # exit
ax_top.text(3.05, 1.7, "main reaches end of code", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax_top.text(1.05, 0.7, "non-daemon worker (program waits → join)", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax_top.text(6.1, 1.2, "program\nexits", color=COLORS["killed"], fontsize=9)
ax_top.set_yticks([0.9, 1.9]); ax_top.set_yticklabels(["worker", "main"])
ax_top.set_title("Non-daemon: the program cannot exit until the worker finishes", fontsize=10)

# --- daemon: program exits and kills the worker ---
ax_bot.broken_barh([(0, 3)], (1.6, 0.6), facecolors=COLORS["main"])      # main runs
ax_bot.broken_barh([(1, 2)], (0.6, 0.6), facecolors=COLORS["daemon"])    # daemon runs...
ax_bot.broken_barh([(3, 3)], (0.6, 0.6), facecolors=COLORS["killed"], alpha=0.4)  # ...killed
ax_bot.axvline(3, color=COLORS["killed"], ls="--", lw=2)
ax_bot.text(3.05, 1.7, "main exits", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax_bot.text(1.05, 0.7, "daemon (killed mid-work →)", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax_bot.text(3.1, 1.2, "program\nexits\n(daemon cut off)", color=COLORS["killed"], fontsize=9)
ax_bot.set_yticks([0.9, 1.9]); ax_bot.set_yticklabels(["daemon", "main"])
ax_bot.set_title("Daemon: main exits on its own; the daemon is killed where it stands", fontsize=10)
ax_bot.set_xlabel("time →", fontsize=11)

for ax in (ax_top, ax_bot):
    ax.set_xlim(0, 8); ax.grid(True, axis="x", alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The top row is why a forgotten non-daemon thread *hangs* your program; the bottom is why a daemon's work can be *cut off*. The fix for both — a stop signal plus a bounded join — is Chapter 6.

## Q&A from the field

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## ▶ Q: How many threads can I create? Is there a limit?

Practically, yes. Each thread gets its **own stack** (Chapter 1), typically allocated around **8 MB** of virtual address space on Linux by default — so thousands of threads cost real memory, and the OS imposes limits (`ulimit -u`). More importantly, past a point **more threads make things slower**, not faster: the scheduler spends its time context-switching instead of doing work (oversubscription). Rule of thumb: for I/O-bound work, tens to low hundreds of threads is fine; if you're reaching for thousands, you want **async** (one thread, many awaited waits) instead. For CPU-bound work, more threads than cores never helps (and the GIL caps it at one anyway). Chapter 11 measures exactly where the payoff stops.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## ▶ Q: My script prints everything but then just hangs and never returns to the prompt — why?

Almost certainly a **non-daemon thread still running**. Python waits for every non-daemon thread before exiting, so if you started a worker with an infinite loop (a server, a poll loop) and never gave it a way to stop, the interpreter waits for it *forever*. Three fixes, best last: (1) make it `daemon=True` if its work is truly disposable; (2) give it a stop flag/`Event` and `join()` it before the end (Chapter 6); (3) check `threading.enumerate()` at the end of your program to see *which* thread is keeping you alive. The hang is not a crash — it's Python being faithful to "non-daemon threads must finish."
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## ▶ Q: Should I subclass `Thread` or pass a `target`? I see both everywhere.

Default to **`target`** — less code, and it keeps the *work* separate from the *threading*. Reach for a **subclass** only when the worker genuinely needs to be an *object*: it holds state across its lifetime (counters, buffers), exposes methods (`.stop()`, `.latest_item`), or *is* a long-lived component of your system. Our producer and worker threads qualify on all three counts, which is why the running example subclasses. A good test: if you find yourself passing the same several variables through `args` and threading mutable containers in to collect results, that state wants to live on a subclass instead.
:::

## Exercises

### Exercise 1: Prove `run()` doesn't make a thread

**Task.** Write a function that prints `threading.current_thread().name`. Call it three ways: (a) directly as `fn()`, (b) via a `Thread` with `.run()`, (c) via a `Thread` with `.start()`. Predict which print `MainThread` before running.

**Expected output.** (a) and (b) print `MainThread`; only (c) prints the worker's name.

```{python}
# YOUR CODE HERE
```

### Exercise 2: A worker that returns a result, two ways

**Task.** Compute `sum(range(1_000_000))` in a worker thread and get the answer back in the main thread, **(a)** by subclassing and storing it on `self`, and **(b)** by passing in a one-element list the worker writes to. Confirm both give the same number.

**Hint.** Read the result only *after* `join()` — that's what makes it safe without a lock.

```{python}
# YOUR CODE HERE
```

### Exercise 3 (Pipeline): a producer with a throughput report

**Task.** Extend `ProducerThread` so that after you `stop()` and `join()` it, you can ask it for the **actual average throughput** it achieved (`items_produced / elapsed_seconds`, i.e. items per second). Time it with `time.perf_counter()` (Chapter 1's monotonic clock). Run it for 0.3 s and print the measured rate.

**Hint.** Record a start timestamp in `run()` and an end timestamp when the loop exits; expose `throughput()` as a method read after join.

```{python}
# YOUR CODE HERE
# Expected: a measured items/sec rate close to the target you set.
```

### Exercise 4: Make a hang, then fix it

**Task.** Write a worker with `while True: time.sleep(0.1)` and start it **non-daemon**. Observe (or reason about) why the program would hang at exit. Then fix it two ways: once by making it a daemon, once by adding a stop flag and `join(timeout=1)`.

```{python}
# YOUR CODE HERE
# (Run the hang version in your own terminal, not here — it won't terminate.)
```

## Where this fits

You can now create workers (target or subclass), feed them data (`args`/`kwargs`), retrieve results (off the object, after `join()`), supervise them (`join(timeout)`, `is_alive()`), and choose between foreground and background (`daemon`) — and you've built the pipeline's **producer**.

But notice what we carefully *avoided*: every example so far had threads touch only their **own** data, or had the main thread read a worker's data strictly **after** `join()`. The moment two running threads write the **same** object at the **same** time — say, a shared `items_processed` counter updated by two workers — the convenient shared heap from Chapter 1 turns dangerous.

That collision is the **race condition**, and [Chapter 3](03_race_conditions.qmd) provokes one on purpose: we'll watch a counter end up *wrong*, figure out exactly which machine-level steps interleaved to corrupt it, and pin down the surprising rules of **what is and isn't atomic in Python**. That's the problem every lock in the following chapters exists to solve.
